methodsAttempted.query: site:rutgers.edu Rutgers Newark generative AI policy ChatGPT academic integrity teaching guidance
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search returned official Rutgers IT AI guidance, central AI index, Rutgers teaching and generative AI guidance, OTEAR archived guidance, and Rutgers Libraries AI teaching guide candidates.

methodsAttempted.query: site:rutgers.edu Rutgers generative AI tools privacy data security policy
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search returned the Rutgers IT AI guidance page, the central AI tools page, the Rutgers Libraries guide, and a business-school news item. Only central Rutgers IT sources were used for data/tool claims.

methodsAttempted.query: site:research.rutgers.edu Rutgers AI research guidance generative AI IRB human subjects
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search returned the official Rutgers Office for Research HRPP Guidance Topics page with AI research and consent guidance.

methodsAttempted.query: site:newark.rutgers.edu Rutgers-Newark generative AI policy ChatGPT academic integrity
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search returned only a course syllabus document, not a campus-level Rutgers-Newark AI policy source.

Verified official Rutgers-controlled hosts used or evaluated in this run: it.rutgers.edu, teaching.rutgers.edu, policies.rutgers.edu, research.rutgers.edu, otear.rutgers.edu, libguides.rutgers.edu, ithelp.rutgers.edu, and newark.rutgers.edu.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found current Rutgers-wide AI guidance from OIT, the central Rutgers AI tools index, Rutgers University Policy Library Academic Integrity Policy 10.2.13, Rutgers Institute for Teaching, Innovation, and Inclusive Pedagogy guidance, and Rutgers Office for Research HRPP AI guidance. The extracted claims are scoped to Rutgers official sources that apply across Rutgers, including Rutgers University-Newark as part of Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey. Archived, library-guide, robots-blocked, school-specific, and course-level sources were not used for policy claims.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers OIT guidance says only Rutgers-approved AI tools should be used at the university to protect university data and ensure appropriate use; Rutgers-approved versions include security and privacy safeguards and do not use Rutgers data to train underlying models.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers OIT guidance says AI use in academic settings typically varies by discipline, course, or instructor; students should not consider AI tools permissible for coursework unless instructors clearly state or communicate permission.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers IT's central AI page lists Microsoft Copilot Chat, NotebookLM, and Google Gemini as centrally funded AI tools available to Rutgers students, faculty, and staff at no additional cost, while Microsoft 365 Copilot, ChatGPT Edu, and Google AI Pro are listed as subscription-required tools.

Rutgers Academic Integrity Policy 10.2.13 applies to all schools and academic units and says students must ensure submitted course, research, or other academic work is their own and created without impermissible technologies, materials, or collaborations.

Rutgers OIT guidance says confidential information, protected health information, and other proprietary Rutgers information may not be appropriate for use in AI applications and systems, and directs users to Rutgers data-classification and IT policies.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers OIT guidance says anyone considering purchase of an AI application for use at Rutgers must follow the same security processes and risk assessments as other software purchases, as well as Rutgers digital-accessibility standards.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers teaching guidance recommends clear and transparent course policies around generative AI, class discussion of those policies and rationales, and, when GenAI is permissible, student disclosure and reflection on use plus submission of prompts and outputs.

citation.publisher: Rutgers Institute for Teaching, Innovation, and Inclusive Pedagogy

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers teaching guidance says GenAI detectors should be treated with caution and cites research indicating they cannot currently be recommended for determining academic-integrity violations because of accuracy limits and false-accusation risk.

citation.publisher: Rutgers Institute for Teaching, Innovation, and Inclusive Pedagogy

artifacts.claimText: Rutgers HRPP guidance says researchers using AI in human-subject research must clearly explain in consent forms how AI will be used, what data it will access, and associated risks and limitations.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The tools licensed by Rutgers include security and privacy safeguards for appropriate use at the university. By using the Rutgers-approved versions of these tools, your data will not be used to train the models underlying the tools. To protect university data and ensure appropriate use, only Rutgers-approved AI tools should be used at the university.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Though AI tools are widely available to students, they should not be considered permissible for coursework unless clearly stated or communicated by instructors. Students are responsible for understanding and abiding by their program and instructors' guidance or rules on the use of AI.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Ensure academic integrity when using AI

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: AI tools (no additional cost): Microsoft Copilot Chat; NotebookLM; Google Gemini. AI tools (subscription required): Microsoft 365 Copilot (apps); ChatGPT Edu; Google AI Pro.

This Academic Integrity Policy applies to all schools and academic units of Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey. The principles of academic integrity require that a student make sure that all work submitted in a course, academic research, or other activity is the student's own and created without the aid of impermissible technologies, materials, or collaborations.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Confidential information, Protected Health Information (PHI), and other proprietary Rutgers information may not be appropriate for use in AI applications and systems. For additional guidance, please consult this data classification chart for AI tools, as well as the Information Classification Policy 70.1.2 and other Information Technology policies.

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: If you are considering the purchase of an AI application for use at Rutgers, you must follow the same security processes and risk assessments as for other software purchases, as well as standards for digital accessibility.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Follow required processes when purchasing AI technology

citation.citationTitle: Guidance on the use of AI at Rutgers

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Have clear and transparent policies around generative artificial intelligence and discuss them with each class as well as the rationales behind them. If utilizing GenAI is permissible in a course, in addition to having students disclose usage, have students reflect on how they used it and how it impacted their learning. In addition, have students turn in any prompts and outputs.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Adopting assignment policies on GenAI / Requesting GenAI usage reflection statements, prompts, and outputs

citation.publisher: Rutgers Institute for Teaching, Innovation, and Inclusive Pedagogy

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The varying performances of GenAI tools and detectors indicate they cannot currently be recommended for determining academic integrity violations due to accuracy limitations and the potential for false accusation which undermines inclusive and fair assessment practices.

citation.publisher: Rutgers Institute for Teaching, Innovation, and Inclusive Pedagogy

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: When using AI in research, whether as a supporting investigative tool or a technology with which human subjects directly interact, researchers must clearly explain in consent forms how AI will be used, what kinds of data it will access, and explicitly outline any associated risks and limitations.

artifacts.notes: Approved as a narrow Rutgers OIT tool-treatment claim. It uses should-language from the source and does not assert approval for every course, use case, or data type.
Approved as academic-integrity guidance scoped to coursework permission depending on instructor or program rules.
Approved as a current tool-availability claim from the Rutgers IT AI index, not as universal course or data-use permission.
Approved because the formal policy states applicability to all Rutgers schools and academic units and expressly references impermissible technologies.
Approved using the source's may-not-be-appropriate language. The robots-disallowed ITHelp data matrix was not used as evidence.
Approved as procurement/security-review guidance because the source uses must-language for AI application purchases.
Approved as teaching guidance from the Rutgers teaching center; not converted into a universal student-use rule.
Approved as cautionary teaching/assessment guidance on GenAI detectors, not as a binding ban on detector use.
Approved as HRPP research guidance for AI use in human-subject research and consent forms.

artifacts.title: Rutgers University-Newark AI Policy Staged Crawl
artifacts.summary: The staged crawl found Rutgers-wide official sources that apply across Rutgers schools and academic units, including Rutgers University-Newark. OIT guidance says only Rutgers-approved AI tools should be used at the university, AI coursework permission depends on instructor or program guidance, confidential or protected data may not be appropriate for AI systems, and AI purchases must follow security/risk and accessibility processes. The central IT AI page lists available tools. Rutgers Academic Integrity Policy 10.2.13 addresses impermissible technologies. Rutgers teaching guidance recommends transparent GenAI course policies and cautions against relying on detectors. Rutgers HRPP guidance requires AI use, data access, risks, and limitations to be explained in human-subject research consent forms.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
The run found Rutgers-wide AI and academic-integrity guidance rather than a distinct Rutgers-Newark campus AI policy page.
Archived, library-guide, robots-disallowed, school-specific, news-only, and course-syllabus sources were not used for policy claims.
Claim candidates are staged for review and are not canonical public claims.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
